name: watchfiles
what_it_does: Simple, modern, fast file watching library for Python, built on Rust's notify crate via PyO3. Provides both sync and async APIs for watching file system changes. Used as the file watcher in Uvicorn (--reload), FastAPI dev server, and many Python development tools. Significantly faster and more reliable than watchdog for Python async contexts.
use_cases: Implement hot-reload in Python development servers using watch() or awatch() async generator for file change events
Build agent systems that monitor configuration file changes and reload without process restart
Watch directories for new files in Python pipelines (inbox processors, file-based triggers) with async support
Implement file synchronization tools that react to change events with cross-platform native OS event support
Replace watchdog in async Python applications where asyncio event loop integration is required
not_for: Python < 3.8 environments — watchfiles requires Python 3.8+ and uses modern async features
Non-Python environments — watchfiles is Python-only; use chokidar for Node.js or notify-rs directly for Rust
Network filesystem watching — native OS events don't work on NFS/CIFS; polling mode may be needed
best_when: You need fast, async-native file watching in Python — especially in FastAPI/Uvicorn/asyncio contexts where watchdog's threading model causes issues.
avoid_when: You need Python < 3.8 support or are watching network filesystems where native OS events are unreliable.

auth.notes: Local filesystem library. No authentication required.

pricing.notes: MIT license. Created by Samuel Colvin (author of Pydantic).

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local filesystem library. Rust backend provides native OS event latency.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Clear error messages for invalid paths and permission issues. Async generator pattern is intuitive. Exceptions propagate cleanly.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: File watching is event-driven — events are reported once per change. Restart watching is straightforward.
known_agent_gotchas: awatch() is an async generator — must be consumed with async for; forgetting await or async for causes the watcher to never start
File change events may be batched — multiple rapid changes to the same file appear as one event; debounce logic may be needed for write-then-read patterns
Watching large directory trees (thousands of files) consumes inotify watches on Linux — check /proc/sys/fs/inotify/max_user_watches and increase if needed
watchfiles detects Python file changes by default for --reload use case — configure filters with Change.added, Change.modified, Change.deleted to customize
Process cancellation must be handled carefully with async generators — use try/finally or async with contextlib.asynccontextmanager for cleanup
Network filesystems (NFS, Samba) do not generate native OS events — watchfiles will miss changes on network mounts without polling mode

security_score_components.security_notes: Local filesystem library. Rust backend provides memory safety. No network calls. Watch paths should be validated to prevent watching sensitive system directories.
